stories.title: Unpaid contractors cloud Obama Center’s finish line
stories.excerpt: Contractors on the Obama Presidential Center say their invoices are going unpaid, reportedly missing millions in payments and jeopardizing the future of businesses.  The Obama Center has gone hundreds of millions over its original $300 million cost estimate. The 19-acre museum and library’s construction cost has ballooned over the years to a staggering $850 million, nearly tripling the projected budget.  African American Contractors Association president Omar Shareef said that a total of seven separate subcontractors have contacted him for help with pursuing missing payments in the past several months, according to Crain’s. Some of the contractors are owed seven figures. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Center ($850M total cost) missed contractor payments; 7+ subcontractors owed millions.
Project tripled original $300M budget due to pandemic and 5-year legal delays.
Construction completed June 2024; systemic contractor/payment management failures evident.
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center in Chicago, which ballooned from $300 million to $850 million, has left multiple subcontractors unpaid—some owing seven figures—as the facility prepares to open. The delays stemmed from pandemic disruptions and a protracted legal battle. At least seven subcontractors have sought help, with some willing to accept losses just to stabilize their businesses, raising serious questions about project financial controls and payment hierarchies.
stories.impact: Large-scale, publicly visible project failures on cost control and contractor payment set a cautionary precedent for major Chicago development. Reputational risk and contractor reluctance to bid on complex civic/institutional projects in Chicago metro may increase labor premiums and timeline risks for future multifamily developers on large mixed-use or amenity-heavy deals.

stories.title: Clovis 7-Eleven C-Store/Gas Station Trades in 1031 Exchange
stories.excerpt: Hanley Investment Group Real Estate Advisors completed the $5-million sale of a 7‑Eleven-occupied convenience store and gas station in Clovis. Within the past 12 months, Hanley has closed more than $60 million in corporate convenience store and gas station deals across California. VP Jacob Fahner and EVP Jeremy McChesney represented a Los Angeles–based private investor in the sale of the single‑tenant, fee‑simple 7‑Eleven located at 394 W. Ashlan Ave. The buyer, a 1031 exchange investor from Bakersfield, was represented by Jeffrey Leggio of ASU Commercial in Bakersfield. “This particular 7‑Eleven offered a rare combination of compelling fundamentals — eligibility for 100% bonus depreciation at a refreshingly accessible $5 million price point, a true absolute triple‑net lease structure, and dominant performance from both the tenant and the grocer anchor,” Fahner said. “Those are exactly the deal nuances that resonate with private investors seeking durable income, and when they are clearly articulated, they drive outcomes like this.” The post Clovis 7-Eleven C-Store/Gas Station Trades in 1031 Exchange appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Clovis, CA: 7-Eleven sale $5M via 1031 exchange to Bakersfield investor.
Hanley Investment Group closed $60M+ in CA convenience/gas deals in 12 months.
Single-tenant net-lease structure with bonus depreciation attracted investor liquidity.
stories.summary: A $5 million 7-Eleven property in Clovis traded via 1031 exchange, purchased by a Bakersfield investor seeking tax-deferred reinvestment. The deal highlighted triple-net lease fundamentals and 100% bonus depreciation eligibility. Hanley's $60 million in similar deals over 12 months in California signals active capital recycling into single-tenant net-lease assets.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, robust 1031 exchange and net-lease market activity in California suggests sustained investor appetite for stable, long-term income assets—a signal that cap rate compression and institutional capital competition remain intense, making ground-up multifamily development sites more expensive in growth corridors like greater Fresno/Clovis.

stories.title: Pet Food Manufacturer Signs 163K-SF Lease in Ontario
stories.excerpt: NAI Capital Commercial VP Dawn Lowder, Julie Dammeier of First Team Real Estate and Andrew Rodriguez represented the landlord in the lease of a 163,336-square-foot industrial facility located at 1930 S. Parco Ave. in Ontario. The tenant, Hoa’s Global Pet Nutrition Inc., was represented by Jesse and Jacklyn Hamilton of Maple Commercial Real Estate. Hoa’s Global Pet will occupy the M2-zoned property, which features heavy power, a fully fenced perimeter, and sits on 8.21 acres. The facility offers flexibility for a variety of industrial uses, including warehousing, distribution, manufacturing, and logistics. Features include fully built-out office space, conference rooms, a training room, and a 10,000-square-foot Class 8 Clean Room. “This facility stands apart in the Inland Empire market,” said Lowder. “The combination of scale, power infrastructure, the Class 8 Clean Room, and freeway and airport access made it an ideal fit for Hoa’s Global Pet Nutrition as the company expands into this next chapter of growth.” The post Pet Food Manufacturer Signs 163K-SF Lease in Ontario appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hoa's Global Pet Nutrition signed 163K SF lease in Ontario (Inland Empire).
Facility features Class 8 clean room, heavy power, 8.21 acres; M2-zoned.
Pet nutrition/manufacturing expansion into IE logistics hub signals industrial demand strength.
stories.summary: Hoa's Global Pet Nutrition leased a 163,336-square-foot industrial facility in Ontario, California, with specialized infrastructure including a 10,000-SF Class 8 clean room, heavy power, and freeway/airport access. The tenant is scaling production operations in the Inland Empire. The property offers flexibility for warehousing, distribution, manufacturing, and logistics.
stories.impact: Ontario/Inland Empire industrial strength—particularly in light-manufacturing and specialized logistics—signals sustained employment growth and labor demand that underpins regional multifamily absorption. Pet food and consumer goods manufacturing expansion drives job stability and wage support in the IE, supporting rent growth in nearby multifamily developments serving essential workers and blue-collar demographics.

stories.title: Former Performing Arts School Sells to Summa Academy as Its Third Bay Area Campus
stories.excerpt: Colliers completed the sale of 2730 Mitchell Dr., a 43,702-square-foot school facility in Walnut Creek, formerly the site of the Contra Costa School of Performing Arts. The buyer, Summa Academy, acquired the asset for $7.1 million. Summa Academy is a non-public school dedicated to serving students (K-22) with autism and other special needs through individualized education and life-skills development. The Walnut Creek location is the largest of Summa Academy’s three East Bay schools. With this location, the organization has room to expand and provide creative space for its students, including a mock apartment with a laundry room and a kitchen. Colliers managing director Scott Ellis, EVP Larry Easterly and associate Charles Easterly represented Contra Costa Performing Arts School. Larry Easterly and SVP Ben Harrison represented Summa Academy. Given 2730 Mitchell Dr.’s former use, “it was important to us to preserve the site as an educational resource for the community,” said Larry Easterly. The post Former Performing Arts School Sells to Summa Academy as Its Third Bay Area Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Summa Academy purchased Walnut Creek school facility for $7.1M; 43.7K SF.
Buyer is special-needs K-22 education provider; third East Bay campus.
Conversion from performing arts to special-education use; community asset preserved.
stories.summary: Summa Academy, a non-public school serving students K-22 with autism and special needs, acquired a 43,702-square-foot former performing arts facility in Walnut Creek for $7.1 million. The property includes office space, mock apartment modules (laundry, kitchen), and room for expansion. This is Summa's third location in the East Bay.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, Walnut Creek's continued redevelopment of aging educational/civic assets signals suburban East Bay market health and institutional investment in workforce development, which may support longer-term population and family formation in the submarket. No bearing on rental demand or development feasibility for market-rate apartments.

stories.title: Sentinel Real Estate Acquires Luxury Apartment Community in Potomac
stories.excerpt: Sentinel Real Estate has acquired The Perry, a luxury multifamily community located at 12430 Park Potomac Avenue in Potomac, Maryland, approximately 10 miles northwest of downtown Washington, D.C. Featuring modern construction, spacious floor plans and high-end interior finishes complemented by a comprehensive indoor and outdoor amenities, The Perry is positioned as one of the premier multifamily communities in the Potomac submarket, which is considered one of the most affluent and desirable submarkets in the U.S. Completed in 2016, The Perry consists of two five-story buildings with a total of 297 units and 8,610 square feet of ground-floor retail space. The property offers a mix of one-, two-, and three-bedroom residences. “The Perry provided a rare opportunity to invest in a residential community that benefits from its irreplaceable location in one of the most sought-after market areas in the country,” said Sentinel’s Managing Director George Tietjen. The post Sentinel Real Estate Acquires Luxury Apartment Community in Potomac appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sentinel Real Estate acquired The Perry (Potomac, MD) for undisclosed price; 297 units.
2016 completion; two 5-story buildings + 8.6K SF retail; 1-, 2-, 3-bed mix.
Potomac is affluent DC submarket; prime location 10 miles NW of downtown.
stories.summary: Sentinel Real Estate acquired The Perry, a 297-unit luxury multifamily community in Potomac, Maryland, completed in 2016. The property features two five-story buildings with modern finishes, ground-floor retail, and a mix of unit sizes. Potomac is one of the nation's most affluent and sought-after residential submarkets proximate to Washington, D.C.
stories.impact: Institutional investor confidence in Potomac luxury multifamily remains strong—the submarket's scarcity of developable land, high barriers to entry, and affluent tenant base insulate Class A assets from downside risk. For developers, this signals limited new supply opportunity and high acquisition costs for stabilized assets; ground-up development in adjacent but less-desirable DC submarkets may offer better risk-adjusted returns.

stories.title: Vornado Closes on 49% Stake in Fisher Brothers’ Park Avenue Plaza
stories.excerpt: Vornado Realty Trust has completed its previously announced acquisition of a 49% interest in Park Avenue Plaza. The 1.2-million-square-foot office property at 55 E. 52nd St. traded at a gross valuation of $1.1 billion, a significant discount to replacement cost. The trophy, Class A office building, co-owned by Fisher Brothers, has protected Park Avenue views and occupies the full through-block between East 52nd and East 53rd streets. The property is located directly across 52nd Street from Vornado’s 350 Park Ave,e development. Park Avenue Plaza is 99% occupied by blue-chip tenants with an 11-year weighted-average lease term and substantially below-market rents. Vornado acquired its interest subject to its share of the $575-million loan encumbering the property that bears interest at a fixed rate of 2.99% and matures in November 2031. Fisher Brothers retains its current 51% ownership interest and continues to manage and lease the property. Vornado and Fisher Brothers have joint control over major decisions. The post Vornado Closes on 49% Stake in Fisher Brothers’ Park Avenue Plaza appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Vornado acquired 49% stake in Park Avenue Plaza (NYC); $1.1B gross valuation.
1.2M SF trophy Class A office; 99% occupied, 11-year WALT, below-market rents.
Subject to $575M loan at 2.99% fixed through Nov 2031; co-ownership with Fisher.
stories.summary: Vornado Realty Trust acquired a 49% interest in Park Avenue Plaza, a 1.2-million-square-foot Class A office building at 55 E. 52nd Street in Manhattan, at a gross valuation of $1.1 billion—a significant discount to replacement cost. The property is 99% occupied by blue-chip tenants with an 11-year weighted-average lease term and rents well below market. Vornado assumed its share of a $575 million loan bearing 2.99% fixed interest through November 2031.
stories.impact: Office acquisition at distressed valuations and low fixed-rate debt indicates Vornado's confidence in eventual NYC office recovery, but also signals continued structural headwinds in Manhattan office (below-market rents, limited pricing power). For multifamily developers in NYC, this highlights competition for capital and institutional investor preference for office repositioning/stabilization over new multifamily development—a constraint on construction financing availability and land pricing in competitive submarkets.

stories.title: Tempo Signs HQ Lease in San Diego to Expand Industrial Electrification
stories.excerpt: A thermochemical energy company that provides power on demand for heavy industrial users and operators has moved its corporate base to San Diego as it transitions to commercial scale. Tempo signed a lease to take over a 35,000-square-foot facility at 6680 Cobra Way in San Diego’s Sorrento Mesa neighborhood. Montana Avenue Capital Partners owns the asset, records show. Tempo says it will utilize the facility for materials development, engineering, prototype assembly and system testing, and ultimately storing and delivering electricity as thermochemical energy. It helps the cement, chemicals and metals industries, among others, replace combustion and fossil fuels with electrified heat. Tempo said it is also finalizing global gigawatt-scale production capabilities. “Our new headquarters is built with the infrastructure required for scale,” Pasquale Romano, CEO of Tempo, said in a statement. “Industrial electrification is moving to broad deployments, and success depends on factory-built, repeatable products. By delivering high-grade heat at a levelized cost of storage lower than lithium iron phosphate systems, we are making the transition from natural gas to electrified heat an economic ‘no-brainer’ for heavy industry.” JLL’s Joe Anderson and Greg Bisconti represented Tempo on the lease. The landlord did not immediately return a request for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Tempo (thermochemical energy/industrial electrification) leased 35K SF HQ in San Diego Sorrento Mesa.
Facility for materials development, engineering, prototype assembly, system testing.
Company scales to gigawatt-scale production; services cement, chemicals, metals industries.
stories.summary: Tempo, an industrial electrification company providing on-demand power for heavy industrial users, signed a 35,000-square-foot lease at 6680 Cobra Way in San Diego's Sorrento Mesa neighborhood to serve as its corporate headquarters. The facility will support materials development, engineering, prototype assembly, and system testing as Tempo scales toward gigawatt-scale production capabilities. Tempo helps industries replace fossil fuels with electrified heat.
stories.impact: San Diego Sorrento Mesa industrial/flex market strength driven by cleantech and advanced manufacturing. Tempo's HQ relocation and production scale-up signal job creation (skilled engineering/manufacturing roles) and sustained industrial demand in San Diego, supporting regional multifamily absorption among white-collar and technical talent. Future expansion could drive demand for transit-oriented or mixed-use developments near tech hubs.

stories.title: From “Pompa-no to Pompa-now”: Mapping Pompano’s latest development boom
stories.excerpt: Pompano Beach has woken up to a building boom. The once-sleepy beach town is seeing a surge in new developments as an influx of luxury projects reshape the city’s landscape. Developers have made Pompano Beach the new horizon, bringing brands like the Ritz-Carlton and Waldorf Astoria to the coast, which offers prime South Florida living without Miami or Palm Beach prices.  Over a dozen major developments are underway in the area, including luxury condominiums, a revived downtown and massive apartment complexes. They’re contributing to a citywide transformation that’s drawing more eyes and more money to Pompano Beach.  This map shows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pompano Beach surge: 12+ major developments underway; Ritz-Carlton, Waldorf Astoria branded.
Fortune International/Oak Capital built Ritz-Carlton (205 units) with $259M construction loan (2023).
Oceanfront scarcity in South Florida + city cooperation driving luxury residential migration from Miami/Palm Beach.
stories.summary: Pompano Beach is experiencing a major development boom with over a dozen luxury residential, commercial, and civic projects underway. Fortune International Group and Oak Capital developed the Ritz-Carlton Residences Pompano Beach (205 luxury units) with a $259 million construction loan from Bank OZK in 2023. The influx of branded luxury (Ritz-Carlton, Waldorf Astoria) is reshaping Pompano as a lower-cost alternative to Miami and Palm Beach, leveraging limited oceanfront land availability and city development cooperation.
stories.impact: Pompano Beach's emergence as a luxury residential destination is cannibalizing high-end demand from Miami and Palm Beach, signaling demographic/wealth shift southward in South Florida coastal markets. Multifamily developers should monitor: (1) whether luxury supply in Pompano saturates and retreats pricing to mid-market levels; (2) spillover demand into secondary/tertiary Broward communities; and (3) potential construction cost inflation driven by competition for skilled trades on Pompano projects.

stories.title: L.A. Mayor ‘Has Grave Concerns’ Amid Allegations at Homeless Agency
stories.excerpt: The Trump administration suspended funding for a prominent Los Angeles homeless services agency due to what federal officials say are failures and a “clear pattern of fraud” in the nation’s capital for homelessness. The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) said the Los Angeles Homeless Services Authority (LAHSA) may have violated federal law and alleged irresponsible actions and failures, including a “lack of financial management, internal controls and safeguards against conflicts of interest.” “HUD has determined it cannot reasonably rely on LAHSA’s ability to operationally and effectively coordinate, manage, distribute and monitor taxpayer dollars in a responsible manner,” an announcement letter read. “LAHSA’s repeated false statements, actions and inactions represent serious violations of multiple HUD regulations and programmatic requirements, and are highly irresponsible. “Los Angeles is, and for years has been, the epicenter of the homelessness crisis in America,” the letter adds. HUD said funding will be suspended immediately, pending an investigation, that could result in funding being restored or permanently stopped. LAHSA has received more than $1.16 billion in public funds over the last five years, according to HUD, and federal funding made up 8 percent of its annual budget this year, according to the L.A. Times. LAHSA said it is working on the alleged mismanagement, and said HUD’s action could “put thousands of formerly homeless people back on the street.” “This appears to be a blatant attempt to pull yet more resources from Los Angeles, a city they have targeted time and again, when it is clear that LAHSA has either corrected or is in the process of correcting nearly all of the issues raised,” the agency said in a statement sent to CO. “Local oversight actions have already resulted in strong repairs and reforms to LAHSA’s internal controls, which are accountable and viewable to the public.” LAHSA added that
bullets: HUD suspended funding to LA Homeless Services Authority (LAHSA) for alleged fraud/mismanagement.
LAHSA cited for lack of financial controls, conflicts of interest, false statements.
LA Mayor Karen Bass expressed grave concerns; impacts homeless services coordination across region.
stories.summary: The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development suspended federal funding to the Los Angeles Homeless Services Authority due to allegations of fraud, financial mismanagement, lack of internal controls, and conflicts of interest. HUD determined it could not rely on LAHSA to responsibly coordinate, manage, and monitor federal homelessness funding. LA Mayor Karen Bass expressed grave concerns about the findings.
stories.impact: Governance crisis at LAHSA undermines regional homelessness services and may worsen street conditions in LA metro. For multifamily developers, increased homelessness and deteriorating urban quality-of-life depress consumer confidence and discourage high-income inflow into dense LA neighborhoods, reducing demand premiums for new market-rate apartment construction. Properties in downtown LA, Hollywood, and Central LA face potential demand headwinds and higher vacancy risk as affluent renters seek safer suburban alternatives.

stories.title: Paul Singer’s Elliott Management Buys Coconut Grove Hotel for $110M: Updated
stories.excerpt: Paul Singer’s Elliott Management bought a hotel in Miami’s Coconut Grove neighborhood from Brookfield Asset Management. Called the Mayfair House Hotel & Garden, the property was built in 1982 and includes 180 rooms at 3000 Florida Avenue, adjacent to Virginia Street. The hotel traded for roughly $110 million, according to a representative for Brookfield, which had purchased the asset for about $40 million in 2019, The Real Deal reported. A recent deed filed in Miami-Dade County lists the sale price at $69.4 million, which likely only included the value of the real estate and excluded the property’s furniture and operating assets. Acore Capital provided a $79 million loan to the West Palm Beach-based buyer. In recent years, Elliott Management, which made a name for itself as a bare-knuckle activist investor, has stepped up its real estate holdings. In 2024, the hedge fund paid $450 million for the 32-story 701 Brickell office building. That same year, it also bought 230-room Gates Hotel South Beach. The firm is also a backer of Adi Chugh’s Tyko Capital, which has emerged as a major source of funding for uxury projects across the country, including one in Coconut Grove. Earlier this year, the lender provided a $410 million construction loan for Terra’s 194-unit boutique condo development. Representatives for Elliott Management did not immediately provide comment. Updated: The story was updated to include the $110 million sale figure. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Elliott Management (Paul Singer) acquired Mayfair House Hotel (Miami Coconut Grove) for ~$110M.
180-room hotel; Elliott paid ~$40M more than Brookfield's 2019 purchase; refinance debt $79M.
Elliott holding real estate portfolio (701 Brickell office, Gates Hotel South Beach); active in Miami market.
stories.summary: Elliott Management, Paul Singer's hedge fund, acquired the Mayfair House Hotel & Garden in Miami's Coconut Grove neighborhood for approximately $110 million from Brookfield Asset Management. The 180-room property, built in 1982, traded at a significant premium to Brookfield's ~$40 million 2019 acquisition price. Elliott financed the deal with a $79 million loan from Acore Capital and is building a broader real estate portfolio in Miami (701 Brickell office, Gates Hotel South Beach).
stories.impact: Hedge fund capital (Elliott Management) aggressively entering Miami real estate signals institutional conviction on South Florida market recovery and long-term upside. However, Elliott's focus on hotel/office repositioning and value-add indicates disciplined capital allocation favoring near-term yield + upside over speculative new development. For multifamily: elevated institutional competition for trophy assets may increase land pricing, but new apartment development financing remains selective and cap-rate dependent.

stories.title: Billions flow across data center deals despite headwinds
stories.excerpt: For all of the controversy dogging data centers these days — prompting municipalities to impose moratoriums on their development — there sure is a lot of money flowing around the sector. In recent days, KKR launched Helix Digital Infrastructure, a developer and operator for the property type, Bisnow reported. The company, which counts Nvidia and Kuwait’s sovereign wealth funds among its partners, kicked off with more than $10 billion in capital commitments. Former Amazon Web Services Chief Executive Officer Adam Selipsky is leading the business, while Nvidia will support chip deployment and Texas-based Vistra will serve as the preferred power […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: KKR launched Helix Digital Infrastructure with $10B capital; ex-AWS CEO leading; Nvidia + Kuwait SWF as partners.
NTT Global Data Centers seeking $1B to fund US projects; 40+ developments, capacity doubling in 2 years.
Data center sector attracts massive capital despite municipal moratoriums and environmental concerns.
stories.summary: KKR launched Helix Digital Infrastructure with over $10 billion in capital commitments to develop and operate data centers, partnering with Nvidia, Kuwait's sovereign wealth fund, and Texas power provider Vistra. Former AWS CEO Adam Selipsky leads the business. Meanwhile, Japan-based NTT Global Data Centers is raising at least $1 billion via Citigroup to fund U.S. projects, with nearly three dozen developments in progress aimed at doubling capacity within two years.
stories.impact: Massive data center capital deployment (KKR $10B+ and NTT $1B+) signals sustained AI/cloud infrastructure demand despite municipal pushback. For multifamily developers in target data center markets (Arizona, Oregon, Texas, Carolinas): expect labor and material cost inflation as construction demand intensifies; utility and power grid constraints may also limit site feasibility. Secondary benefit: data center employment/payroll growth in regional markets supports multifamily demand, but developer competition for land and labor will remain fierce.

stories.title: AMAC Holdings Sells Adjacent East Village Buildings for $23.5M
stories.excerpt: AMAC Holdings’ Maurice Kaufman has sold two adjacent buildings in Manhattan’s East Village for a combined $23.5 million, according to property records made public Thursday. Investment, management and development firm AG Paratus bought the two multifamily properties at 111 East 12th Street and 113 East 12th Street, the deed transfer shows. AMAC sold the properties under the limited liability company names AH 10 Rutgers and 111-113 E 12th Property LLC, with Kaufman as the signatory. Jason Halpern, founder of AG Paratus’ frequent partner JMH Development, signed for the buyer. Spokespeople for AG Paratus and AMAC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. AMAC purchased the buildings for $20 million in June 2018, with the entities masked behind law firms and attorney signatories. The six-story 111 East 12th Street was constructed in 1900 and has 11 apartments with a retail unit, which seems to be occupied by marketing consultant Grand Central Marketing. Next-door 113 East 12th Street is a five-story building from about the same period meant for four to five families, the deed indicates. Through AMAC Development, Kaufman’s firm has been busy on a national level. His firm secured a $34 million loan from Affinius Capital to refinance a multifamily asset in suburban Dallas in February 2025, and he also began development on a 290-unit apartment complex at 17990 West Dixie Highway in Aventura, Fla., in 2022. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: AMAC Holdings sold two East Village buildings (111 & 113 East 12th St) to AG Paratus for $23.5M.
Sold for $3.5M profit vs. June 2018 purchase ($20M); 1900s vintage, 11 + 4–5 unit buildings.
Manhattan East Village multifamily trading; modest value appreciation over 6-year hold.
stories.summary: AMAC Holdings (Maurice Kaufman) sold two adjacent historic multifamily buildings in Manhattan's East Village to AG Paratus for $23.5 million. The 111 East 12th Street (1900, 11 apartments + retail) and 113 East 12th Street (1900s, 4–5 family units) traded for a $3.5 million gain over AMAC's June 2018 acquisition price of $20 million. JMH Development partnered with AG Paratus on the acquisition.
stories.impact: Modest East Village cap rate compression ($20M to $23.5M in 6 years ≈ 2.8% annual appreciation) suggests East Village rent growth has slowed or investor expectations have moderated. For developers: vintage East Village small multifamily assets are trading at lower appreciation rates than peak 2015–2020; new development costs and acquisition land prices in East Village remain elevated relative to near-term rent growth, making ground-up or major redevelopment projects less attractive.

stories.title: Midwood Pays $41M for West Village Retail Swath at 403 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: A large commercial parcel in the heart of Manhattan’s West Village has changed hands for $41 million. The retail property at 403 Avenue of the Americas was acquired in cash by John Usdan’s Midwood Investment & Development, according to city records made public Friday. The one-story retail buildings sit at the corner of Greenwich Avenue, running toward Christopher Street. The property also includes adjacent 405 Avenue of the Americas and 1 Greenwich Avenue. The family-owned Midwood purchased the collection of storefronts from longtime owner Millsmith, a limited liability company with family ties to real estate firm Samco Properties. Joshua Smith, a partner at Milsmith Real Estate Advisory Services and general counsel at Samco, signed the deed for the seller, records show. Current retail tenants at the West Village property two blocks northwest of Washington Square Park include Italian restaurant Olio E Più, florist Casey’s Flower Studio, fast-food chain Chipotle Mexican Grill and optometrist Cohen’s Fashion Optical. It is unclear whether Midwood or Millsmith worked with an outside brokerage in the transaction. Millsmith, through the entity 405 Sixth, acquired the commercial plot in 2007. Midwood’s new acquisition was split between two entities holding the title as tenants-in-common. One Greenwich Retail ECU, with a 51 percent stake, was signed for by Usdan, while Snapdragon Owner was co-signed by managers Claus Zellner, Jonathan Spice and Philip Dean. The family-owned Midwood operates a national portfolio of development, office, retail and residential properties across several U.S. markets. The retail acquisition adds to their West Village footprint, which includes the 159-unit Candela Tower at 56 Seventh Avenue, acquired from BlackRock for $102 million in 2022. The firm more recently landed a $200 million construction loan for a 21-story, 276-unit multifamily project at 200 Douglass Street in Brooklyn’s Gowanus neighborhood. A spokesperson for Midwood did not im
bullets: Midwood Investment & Development bought 403 Avenue of the Americas (West Village, NYC) for $41M cash.
Retail/storefront property; tenants include Olio E Più, Chipotle, Casey's Flowers, Cohen's Optical.
Purchased from longtime owner Millsmith (Samco Properties family office); prime West Village location.
stories.summary: Midwood Investment & Development, led by John Usdan, acquired a retail property at 403 Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan's West Village for $41 million in cash. The one-story retail buildings at the corner of Greenwich Avenue and Christopher Street include adjacent properties at 405 Avenue of the Americas and 1 Greenwich Avenue. Current tenants include Olio E Più, Chipotle, Casey's Flower Studio, and Cohen's Fashion Optical.
stories.impact: Midwood's cash acquisition of West Village retail signifies strong capital and investor appetite for trophy retail real estate in NYC's most affluent neighborhoods. For multifamily: retail land/property scarcity and premium valuations in West Village and surrounding Greenwich Village limit residential redevelopment opportunities; soft hotel/office conversions to residential remain more feasible, but face zoning and community opposition. Retail fundamentals in West Village remain resilient, limiting near-term conversion optionality.

stories.title: Musk’s SpaceX eyes 11M sf “Gigasat” factory in Bastrop County
stories.excerpt: With Elon Musk’s real estate and business dealings, two words are always paramount: bigger and more.  Those two driving ideas are on full display with his plans for the new SpaceX campus in Bastrop County, just east of Austin. SpaceX will be expanding the solar production facility on FM 1209 to 11 million square feet, according to the Austin Business Journal. The “Gigasat” will be one million square feet bigger than the Tesla “Gigafactory” just down the road from the Austin-Bergstrom International Airport. The Gigasat will be utilized for a project with common Muskian ambition: sending data centers to space. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: SpaceX plans 11M SF 'Gigasat' factory expansion in Bastrop County (east Austin); satellite/data center manufacturing.
Facility adds solar ingots/wafers and AI satellite production; goal: 6,500 satellites by end 2027.
1M SF larger than Tesla Gigafactory; supports xAI, Tesla, SpaceX scaling on ~1,000 acres.
stories.summary: SpaceX is expanding its Bastrop County facility (east of Austin) to 11 million square feet for satellite and data center manufacturing as part of Elon Musk's plan to deploy AI-powered data centers in orbit by end 2027. The 'Gigasat' will include solar ingots/wafers production and AI satellite assembly, with a goal of launching 6,500 satellites by December 2027. The expansion is larger than Tesla's nearby Gigafactory and leverages approximately 1,000 acres to support manufacturing and production scaling for Musk's companies (xAI, Tesla, SpaceX).
stories.impact: SpaceX's massive Bastrop County industrial expansion will drive significant job creation (engineering, manufacturing, logistics) in greater Austin metro, fueling high-skilled labor inflow and wage growth. Expect multifamily demand surge in East Travis County and suburban Austin corridors closest to SpaceX operations (Bastrop, Elgin, possibly Lockhart). Development window is narrow (2026–2027); developers should acquire land and begin planning mixed-use/workforce housing near Gigasat facility before land costs spike.

stories.title: Charlotte giving red light to data center projects for 150 days
stories.excerpt: The Charlotte City Council voted for a 150-day moratorium on new data center development.  During the development pause, city officials will study the developments’ impact on the environment in order to streamline their policy.  “When we can agree on the facts, then we can talk about good policy,” council member Victoria Watlington said, according to Bisnow.  The pause does not apply to already-greenlit developments with valid building permits and a complete application for developmental approval, the outlet said.  Local residents and leaders have been rallying, specifically, against American Tower Corporation’s 40,000-square-foot data center, nestled next to a nature preserve. A […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Charlotte City Council voted 150-day moratorium on new data center development (effective immediately).
Pause allows study of environmental/community impacts; applies to new proposals (not valid permits).
American Tower Corp 40K SF data center next to nature preserve was catalyst; 43 data centers in Charlotte pipeline.
stories.summary: Charlotte City Council enacted a 150-day moratorium on new data center development to study environmental and community impacts. The pause does not apply to projects with valid building permits and complete developmental approval applications. The moratorium was prompted by resident and city opposition to American Tower Corporation's 40,000-square-foot facility adjacent to a nature preserve. Charlotte has 43 data center projects in the pipeline; North Carolina statewide has 93 projects across 19 markets.
stories.impact: Charlotte's data center moratorium signals growing municipal resistance to rapid data center proliferation due to environmental and power-grid concerns. For multifamily developers: reduced data center investment and employment inflow into Charlotte over next 6–12 months may soften rental demand growth in near term. However, if moratorium leads to permanent restrictions or relocation of projects to surrounding NC communities (Durham, Raleigh, Cary, Apex), multifamily demand may shift to secondary markets with fewer restrictions, creating opportunity for developers in less-regulated submarkets.

stories.title: Hunington plans $33M multifamily project in booming Fulshear
stories.excerpt: Apartments are sprouting in a sea of single-family development outside Houston. An LLC linked to Houston-based Hunington Properties filed plans with the Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation to build a 348-unit multifamily development at 3700 Jordan Ranch Boulevard in Fulshear, a swiftly growing town in Fort Bend County west of Houston. The apartment community will span 444,607 square feet across 12 three-story buildings and a freestanding clubhouse, according to the project filing. Amenities will include a pool court with a cabana, an outdoor exercise area and a dog park. Construction is scheduled to begin September 2026 and end March […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hunington Properties filing 348-unit multifamily project in Fulshear, TX; $33M budgeted; 3700 Jordan Ranch Blvd.
Fulshear ranked 2nd fastest-growing US city (20K+ pop.) in 2025; construction scheduled Sept 2026–Mar 2027.
Within Jordan Ranch MPC (Johnson Development); 16.4 acres; single-family sprawl continuing nearby (Hines 3.7K acres).
stories.summary: Hunington Properties filed plans to develop a 348-unit multifamily project in Fulshear, Texas, west of Houston, at an estimated cost of $33 million. The community will span 444,607 square feet across 12 three-story buildings with amenities including pool/cabana, dog park, and exercise areas. Construction is scheduled for September 2026 through March 2027. Fulshear ranked second nationally among fastest-growing cities with populations over 20,000 last year and is part of Johnson Development's 1,350-acre Jordan Ranch master-planned community.
stories.impact: Fulshear's explosive growth (2nd fastest nationally) driven by proximity to Houston and large-scale master-planned development (Johnson, Hines) is creating sustained multifamily demand. Hunington's 348-unit project signals developer confidence in rent absorption and demographic inflow. For investors/developers: land costs in Fulshear and adjacent Fort Bend County submarkets are appreciating rapidly; acquisition windows are closing. Competition for ground-up multifamily sites will intensify; developers should prioritize land control in Jordan Ranch and competing MPCs before 2027 launches exhaust inventory and bid up pricing.
